content.text: What It Is

License compliance scanning is the automated process of detecting, classifying, and policy-evaluating the software licence attached to every package in an application's dependency graph. For agentic AI applications, this process must cover not only the Python or Node packages in the orchestration layer but also model artefacts, datasets, and OSS tools invoked as agent actions. The scan pipeline ingests a resolved lockfile or container image, extracts licence text from each package, matches it against the SPDX identifier catalogue, evaluates the identified licence against the organisation's allow-list policy, and generates a machine-readable Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) that records the decision and any approval conditions.

The output is a structured compliance record rather than a binary pass/fail signal. It distinguishes between packages that pass unconditionally (permissive licences), packages that pass with conditions (weak-copyleft licences requiring dynamic linking), packages flagged for manual review (unknown or ambiguous identifiers), and packages that fail and block the build (strong-copyleft licences incompatible with the distribution model). This graduated structure allows engineering teams to move fast on the common permissive case while surfacing only genuinely novel licensing questions for legal review.
Why It Matters

The agentic AI ecosystem concentrates OSS exposure in ways conventional applications do not. A single framework update can alter dozens of transitive dependencies; model checkpoints carry licence obligations that differ fundamentally from the serving code that loads them; OSS tool plugins fetched at agent runtime bypass static analysis entirely. Without automated scanning in the build pipeline, licence obligations accumulate silently until a legal audit or a competitor's infringement claim forces a costly retrospective analysis.

The regulatory pressure is also growing. The EU Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) requires manufacturers of products with digital elements to have documented SBOM coverage for all OSS components. The US Executive Order 14028 mandates SBOM disclosure for software sold to the federal government. Enterprise customers increasingly include SBOM provision as a procurement requirement in their vendor contracts. An automated licence compliance pipeline is the only way to meet these obligations at the velocity modern AI product teams ship.
Core Benefits
Early detection — licence violations are caught at the dependency resolution stage in CI, not during legal diligence before a funding round or acquisition.
Consistent policy application — the same SPDX allow-list and policy rules are applied to every build, eliminating developer-to-developer variance in judgements about ambiguous licences.

Audit-ready SBOM — every build produces a CycloneDX or SPDX JSON file that is versioned alongside the release artefact, satisfying regulatory and customer audit requirements without additional manual effort.
Legal cost reduction — automated triage routes only genuinely ambiguous cases to legal counsel, reducing billable time on routine licence checks.
Developer trust — clear, deterministic policy evaluation (not case-by-case legal opinion) lets developers self-serve licence checks without waiting for legal review on common permissive packages.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Heuristic detection uncertainty — scanners that rely on file-header matching or text similarity (ScanCode, LicenseFinder) can misclassify licences with modified boilerplate. Low-confidence detections require manual verification, creating a queue that must be managed.

SPDX coverage gaps — foundation model licences (Llama Community Licence, OpenRAIL-M, BigScience RAIL) are not in the SPDX catalogue. Scanners return 'unknown' for these artefacts, requiring a separate non-SPDX classification workflow.

Point-in-time snapshots — the SBOM reflects the dependency graph at build time. Runtime-installed packages (pip install inside a code-execution sandbox) are not captured. The CycloneDX 'composition completeness' field should be set to 'incomplete' for these deployments.

Multi-licence packages — some packages are dual-licensed (e.g., 'MIT OR Apache-2.0') or contain files under different licences. Scanners must handle SPDX expressions, not just single identifiers; policy evaluation on compound expressions requires expression parsing, not string comparison.
How It Works — The Four-Stage Pipeline

Dependency Extraction: The resolved lockfile (requirements.txt, poetry.lock, package-lock.json) is parsed to enumerate every package at its pinned version. Each package is downloaded from the registry and extracted for licence file inspection. For container images, the layer filesystem is extracted with Syft or similar.

SPDX Classification: A scanner (ScanCode Toolkit, FOSSA agent, or ORT) inspects METADATA files, LICENCE files, and source file headers for each package. The scanner assigns an SPDX identifier and a confidence score. The declared licence in the package manifest is cross-checked against the file-level detection; mismatches are flagged for manual review.

Policy Evaluation: The detected SPDX identifier is looked up in the organisational allow-list, conditional list, and deny-list. The distribution model (SaaS, on-premises, embedded) is a parameter — AGPL is denied for SaaS but may be conditionally permitted for on-premises deployments where source disclosure obligations can be met. The evaluation returns a decision (PASS / CONDITIONAL / FAIL / NEEDS_REVIEW) and an action code that drives CI behaviour.

Report Generation: Passing and conditional components are written to the SBOM registry in CycloneDX 1.5 JSON format. Failing components trigger a build failure and open an automatic ticket in the issue tracker with the licence text, the policy rule that matched, and the recommended remediation (remove, replace, or seek exception). Conditional components have their conditions appended to the SBOM entry as properties.
Architecture
The scanning pipeline integrates into CI as a job that runs after dependency resolution and before the build step:

attrs.alt: License compliance scanning pipeline with four stages (Dependency Extraction, SPDX Classification, Policy Evaluation, Report Generation) leading to a diamond Policy Decision node with three outcome branches: PASS to allow-list match, CONDITIONAL for weak copyleft, FAIL for strong copyleft, all feeding into SBOM and Audit Trail output in CycloneDX/SPDX format

content.text: The policy configuration (allow-list, conditional-list, deny-list) is version-controlled in the governance repository alongside the SBOM registry. Changes to the policy trigger a re-scan of all packages currently in the registry, ensuring that a newly denied licence identifier immediately flags existing approvals for review.
When to Apply
On every pull request that modifies dependency lockfiles — new or version-bumped packages must pass licence scanning before the branch can merge.
On every release build — the SBOM attached to the release artefact must reflect the exact versions shipped, not a cached prior scan.

On a scheduled weekly scan of the full SBOM registry — to catch newly disclosed licence-related issues in approved packages (e.g., an upstream project retroactively changing licence terms, which has occurred in the infrastructure OSS space).
When adding open-weight model checkpoints — model artefacts must be scanned separately since they are not pip packages but carry significant licence obligations.
How to Implement

Choose a scanner appropriate for your stack. OSS Review Toolkit (ORT) supports Python, Node, Go, Java, and Cargo in a single pipeline and outputs CycloneDX natively. FOSSA provides a SaaS version with legal advisory integration. ScanCode Toolkit is the most accurate open-source option for deep file-level scanning.

Version-control your allow/deny/conditional lists as JSON files in the governance repo. Schema: an object mapping SPDX identifiers to {decision, conditions, notes, effective_date}. This gives an audit trail of every policy change.

Integrate the scanner into CI as a blocking job for lockfile-modifying PRs. Use the scanner's exit code to fail the build on any FAIL-decision package. NEEDS_REVIEW decisions should fail the build only if they are on the critical path; non-critical dev-only packages with unresolved licences can be warned without blocking.

Configure SPDX expression parsing, not string matching, for multi-licence packages. ORT includes expression evaluators; implement your own evaluator only if the toolchain does not support expressions.
Establish a manual review SLA for NEEDS_REVIEW packages: 5 business days for runtime dependencies, 15 for dev dependencies. Track the queue in the issue tracker with a dedicated label.
Worked Example — FastAPI + LangChain Build
A team adds fastapi@0.111, langchain-core@0.2.10, and psycopg2-binary@2.9.9 to a SaaS agent's requirements.txt. The CI licence scan runs:
fastapi@0.111 — MIT declared, MIT detected, confidence 0.99. PASS.
langchain-core@0.2.10 — MIT declared, MIT detected, confidence 0.99. PASS.

psycopg2-binary@2.9.9 — LGPL-3.0-or-later declared, LGPL-3.0-or-later detected. This is in the CONDITIONAL list. Decision: CONDITIONAL. Action: 'add_linking_condition_to_sbom'. A CI comment is added to the PR: 'psycopg2-binary is LGPL-3.0. As a binary extension loaded via Python's dynamic linker, it satisfies the LGPL dynamic linking exception. Condition recorded in SBOM entry.' Build proceeds.
Real-World Example — SSPL MongoDB Driver Rejection

In 2018, MongoDB relicensed from AGPL-3.0 to SSPL-1.0 (Server Side Public Licence). SSPL requires that any organisation offering MongoDB as a service must open-source not just the MongoDB modifications but their entire service-delivery stack, including the cloud infrastructure provisioning code. Most OSS licence scanners do not include SSPL in their standard SPDX catalogue, so the MongoDB 4.0+ driver would return a NEEDS_REVIEW result. A properly configured deny-list would include 'SSPL-1.0' as an explicitly blocked identifier. A team that added a MongoDB 5.0 driver to a SaaS agent memory store without a licence scan would inadvertently accept an obligation to publish their proprietary cloud deployment infrastructure. The licence compliance scanner's FAIL result and the resulting build break would surface this obligation before the component reached production.
Common Mistakes

Trusting declared licences without file-level verification — package metadata can be incorrect or out of date. ScanCode regularly finds mismatches between declared and actual licence files, particularly in Python packages with bundled C extensions.

String-matching SPDX IDs — 'GPL-3.0' and 'GPL-3.0-only' and 'GPL-3.0-or-later' are different SPDX identifiers with different obligations. String comparison breaks on minor formatting variants. Always normalise to canonical SPDX IDs before policy evaluation.

Running scans only on direct dependencies — pip's dependency resolver pulls transitive dependencies that are not listed in requirements.in. Always scan the fully resolved lockfile, not the developer-authored requirements file.
Ignoring model and dataset artefacts — treating licence scanning as a 'code packages only' problem misses the most novel and highest-risk licence obligations in agentic AI deployments.

No escalation path for NEEDS_REVIEW decisions — if there is no defined SLA and owner for the manual review queue, packages stay in NEEDS_REVIEW indefinitely and teams start bypassing the gate via exception flags.
Best Practices

Cache scan results by (package, version, SPDX-ID) at the scanner output stage. Licences are immutable per published version; re-scanning the same version is waste. A Redis or SQLite cache makes CI licence gates sub-second for known packages.
Attach the SBOM to every release artefact as a signed attestation using SLSA provenance or in-toto. This proves the SBOM was generated from the same build that produced the artefact, not post-hoc.

Treat the deny-list as an evolving document. Add 'BUSL-1.1' (Business Source Licence — used by HashiCorp) and 'SSPL-1.0' (MongoDB) explicitly, since these are legally ambiguous and scanner coverage is inconsistent.

Separate the model SBOM from the software SBOM. Include fields for model provenance (original dataset licence, training methodology disclosure) alongside the checkpoint file hash and model licence identifier.
Performance and Scalability
ScanCode deep file scanning can take 5-15 minutes per package for large packages with many files. Practical mitigations:

Two-tier scanning: use the declared SPDX ID (from package metadata) for the fast first tier. Only run deep file scanning for packages where the declared ID is absent, low-confidence, or in the conditional/deny list.
Scan new packages only: compare the current lockfile against the prior approved lockfile and scan only the diff. Most PRs add one to three packages, not hundreds.

Parallelise across packages: each package scan is independent. Use concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor to run scans in parallel; typical full-stack scans on 200-package lockfiles complete in under 3 minutes with 8 workers.
Tooling and Ecosystem
OSS Review Toolkit (ORT) — end-to-end licence compliance pipeline supporting 20+ package managers. Outputs CycloneDX and SPDX. Has a rule engine for policy configuration. Open-source, Apache-2.0.
ScanCode Toolkit — most accurate open-source licence text scanner. Detects 1600+ licence types. Used internally by ORT for file-level scanning. Apache-2.0.

FOSSA — commercial SaaS licence compliance platform with legal advisory tier. Integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Jira. Provides ongoing monitoring and alerts when upstream packages change licence. Strong model for enterprise teams without in-house legal expertise.

Syft + Grype — Anchore's open-source SBOM generation and vulnerability scanning suite. Syft generates CycloneDX SBOMs from container images and lockfiles; Grype cross-references against CVE databases. Apache-2.0.

pip-licenses — lightweight Python library for extracting declared licence metadata from installed packages. Suitable for quick checks but not a replacement for file-level scanning of production dependencies.
Comparison with Alternatives
Manual legal review of all dependencies — accurate but completely unscalable beyond a handful of packages. Reserve manual review for NEEDS_REVIEW queue items only.

Metadata-only scanning (declared licence without file verification) — fast and sufficient for the majority of well-maintained packages, but misses bundled C extensions and historically inaccurate metadata. Acceptable only if combined with a file-level rescan for any CONDITIONAL or DENY-list candidates.

Commercial licence intelligence databases (Black Duck, WhiteSource) — comprehensive coverage and continuous monitoring, but expensive for early-stage teams. The managed update cadence (licence changes propagated without triggering manual rescan) is the primary advantage over open-source tooling.
Security and Reliability

Integrity of the scan input — verify package checksums against the registry manifest before scanning. A supply-chain attack that substitutes a malicious package might also alter licence files to evade detection. Pin to checksums in the lockfile and verify at download time.

Policy configuration as code — store allow/deny/conditional lists in the same version-controlled repository as application code. Changes require pull request review and generate an audit entry. Never allow runtime modification of the policy configuration.

Signed SBOM attestation — sign the generated SBOM with the build system's signing key (e.g., using Sigstore cosign). This allows downstream consumers to verify that the SBOM was produced by the authorised CI system, not fabricated after the fact.
Implementation — Code
The following code implements the four-stage scanning pipeline. Stage 1 runs ScanCode on an extracted package directory:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import subprocess
import json

@dataclass
class LicenseScanResult:
    package_name: str
    version: str
    detected_spdx: Optional[str]
    confidence: float          # 0.0 - 1.0
    detection_method: str      # 'declared', 'file_header', 'heuristic'
    raw_text: Optional[str]    # snippet of matched licence text

    @property
    def needs_manual_review(self) -> bool:
        return self.confidence < 0.85 or self.detected_spdx is None

def run_scancode(package_dir: str) -> list[LicenseScanResult]:
    """
    Run ScanCode Toolkit on an extracted package directory.
    Returns structured results for each detected licence.
    """
    result = subprocess.run(
        [
            'scancode', '--license', '--json-pp', '-', '--quiet',
            package_dir,
        ],
        capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120
    )
    raw = json.loads(result.stdout)
    findings = []
    for file_info in raw.get('files', []):
        for lic in file_info.get('licenses', []):
            findings.append(LicenseScanResult(
                package_name=package_dir.rsplit('/', 1)[-1],
                version='unknown',
                detected_spdx=lic.get('spdx_license_key'),
                confidence=lic.get('score', 0) / 100.0,
                detection_method=(
                    'declared' if lic.get('matched_rule', {}).get('is_license_reference')
                    else 'file_header'
                ),
                raw_text=lic.get('matched_text', '')[:200],
            ))
    return findings
Stage 3 evaluates the detected SPDX identifier against the organisational policy lists:
The pipeline orchestrator coordinates all stages and generates the CycloneDX SBOM:

import hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import List

@dataclass
class SBOMEntry:
    package_name: str
    version: str
    spdx_id: str
    decision: PolicyDecision
    conditions: list
    approved_by: str
    approved_at: str
    review_due: str            # ISO date
    checksum_sha256: str       # lockfile-pinned package checksum

class LicenseCompliancePipeline:
    def __init__(self, sbom_path: str, distribution: str):
        self.sbom_path = sbom_path
        self.distribution = distribution
        self._sbom: List[SBOMEntry] = []

    def scan_lockfile(self, lockfile_path: str) -> List[dict]:
        """
        Parse requirements.txt / poetry.lock, scan each package,
        evaluate policy, and return a report per package.
        """
        packages = self._parse_lockfile(lockfile_path)
        report = []
        for pkg in packages:
            scan_results = run_scancode(pkg['extracted_dir'])
            # Consolidate multiple licence detections — take the most restrictive
            dominant = self._dominant_licence(scan_results)
            decision_meta = evaluate_policy(dominant, self.distribution)
            report.append({
                'package': pkg['name'],
                'version': pkg['version'],
                'spdxId': dominant.detected_spdx,
                'confidence': dominant.confidence,
                'decision': decision_meta['decision'].value,
                'reason': decision_meta['reason'],
                'action': decision_meta.get('action'),
            })
        return report

    def _dominant_licence(self, results: List[LicenseScanResult]) -> LicenseScanResult:
        """Return the most restrictive detected licence across all files."""
        priority = list(DENY_LIST) + list(CONDITIONAL_LIST) + list(ALLOW_LIST)
        for priority_spdx in priority:
            for r in results:
                if r.detected_spdx == priority_spdx:
                    return r
        return results[0] if results else LicenseScanResult(
            'unknown', '0', None, 0.0, 'none', None)

    def _parse_lockfile(self, path: str) -> list:
        # Implementation parses pip freeze / poetry lock format
        # and downloads + extracts each package for scanning
        raise NotImplementedError('Use pip-audit or ort for production parsing')

    def generate_cyclonedx_sbom(self, entries: List[SBOMEntry]) -> dict:
        return {
            'bomFormat': 'CycloneDX',
            'specVersion': '1.5',
            'version': 1,
            'metadata': {
                'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + 'Z',
                'component': {'type': 'application', 'name': 'agentic-ai-app'},
            },
            'components': [
                {
                    'type': 'library',
                    'name': e.package_name,
                    'version': e.version,
                    'licenses': [{'license': {'id': e.spdx_id}}],
                    'hashes': [{'alg': 'SHA-256', 'content': e.checksum_sha256}],
                    'externalReferences': [],
                    'properties': [
                        {'name': 'policy:decision', 'value': e.decision.value},
                        {'name': 'policy:conditions', 'value': str(e.conditions)},
                        {'name': 'policy:reviewDue', 'value': e.review_due},
                    ],
                }
                for e in entries
            ],
        }
Key Takeaways

Licence compliance scanning for agentic AI runs a four-stage pipeline: dependency extraction, SPDX classification, policy evaluation, and SBOM generation — with graduated outcomes (PASS, CONDITIONAL, FAIL, NEEDS_REVIEW) rather than a binary gate.

File-level scanning (ScanCode, ORT) must supplement declared-metadata scanning because package metadata is frequently incomplete or inaccurate, especially for packages with bundled compiled extensions.

Foundation model licences (Llama, OpenRAIL-M, SSPL) are not in the SPDX catalogue and require a separate non-standard classification workflow; they must be tracked in a model-specific SBOM alongside the software SBOM.

The CycloneDX 1.5 format with signed attestation is the recommended output format, satisfying EU CRA and EO 14028 SBOM requirements while providing the policy metadata fields needed for ongoing compliance monitoring.

Performance optimisations (result caching by version, diff-only scanning, parallel execution) are essential to keep licence gates under 3 minutes in CI, preventing the compliance scan from becoming a developer bottleneck.
